context.topic: EAF melt shop operations on the 80-tonne furnace
context.gaps_summary: Almost no formal knowledge captured on charging practice, power profile, or off-gas energy. Some scattered notes on slag work but nothing systematic.
context.relevant_rules: R-EAF-005 'Extend EAF refining by 5-8 min when copper exceeds 0.20%' (status: Active)

answers: Charging is everything. We run three buckets per heat on the 80-tonne shell, no four-bucket charges anymore — too much dead time and you lose the hot heel benefit. First bucket is the heavy stuff: 60 percent shred, 30 percent #1 heavy melt, and a 10 percent layer of low-residual prime at the bottom over the hot heel to bridge it. Bridge layer is the thing nobody trains you on — without it your electrodes punch right through into the heel and you get an arc flash. Second bucket goes lighter: 80 percent shred and a layer of pig iron at the top to feed the bath chemistry. Third bucket is whatever cleanup you need plus your alloy adjustments. Never put turnings or borings in the first bucket — they pack down and choke the hot heel mixing. Borings go in the third bucket only, and only if you're below 5 percent of total weight. We had a copper contamination event in 2022 because an operator put a bucket of mixed shred with copper-bearing motor scrap in first — took us six heats to wash the residual out and we lost a customer order on it.

Power profile depends entirely on what stage you're in. Bore-in we run 70 to 80 percent of secondary voltage on tap 7 of 13, low impedance — you want arc length short so it doesn't burn the roof down. Once we have a flat bath, around 25 minutes in for a cold start or 18 minutes for a hot heel start, we crank up to tap 12 or 13 at full secondary, around 950 volts. Long arc on flat bath is where you get your real productivity — 80 to 90 megawatts on this furnace at peak. Critical thing: if the foamy slag isn't covering the arc you have to throttle back, every 100 millimetres of slag layer above the arc tip lets you push another 5 to 10 percent on power. Flicker complaints from the grid mean you're running too short an arc — check your electrode regulator response time, should be under 200 milliseconds for any deviation. We had a stretch in February where the regulator was sluggish at 380 milliseconds and we burned through three roof refractory patches in a month, plus the utility company called us about flicker.

Slag chemistry. Target basicity is 1.8 to 2.2 — that's CaO over SiO2 — for low-carbon plain rebar. For higher-carbon and HSLA we go more basic, 2.4 to 2.6, because phosphorus and sulphur partition better into the slag at higher basicity. FeO content is what controls oxidation potential — we target 18 to 25 percent FeO during refining. Below 15 you can't oxidize the phosphorus and it reverts. Above 30 you're losing iron units and your yield drops. We measure FeO by sampling at the slag door every 10 minutes during refining. The slag colour tells you a lot too — green-grey means you're in the right range, brown-red means too much FeO, dark glassy black means starved on lime addition. Add lime in 200 to 300 kilo charges, never dump a tonne at once because it forms unmelted clumps that take 15 minutes to dissolve. Always inject carbon under the slag, not on top — surface injection just burns off uselessly.

Foaming is the trick that separates good melt shops from average ones. You want 400 to 600 millimetres of foam covering the arc through the entire flat bath stage. Without foam your arc is naked, you're radiating heat into the sidewall instead of the bath, your electrode tip wears 30 percent faster, and your refractory life drops by half. Recipe is carbon injection into the slag — 1.5 to 2 kilos per tonne of carbon at 8 to 12 bar pressure, lance angle 30 to 45 degrees below horizontal pointed at the slag-metal interface. The carbon reduces FeO to iron and CO bubbles, which is what foams the slag. If you're not foaming check three things in order: FeO level — needs to be above 18 percent or there's nothing to reduce; slag basicity — too low and the slag is too fluid to hold the foam; carbon delivery rate — clogged injection lance is the most common cause. Tap the lance physically with a steel rod every 4 hours, you'll feel the difference between a clear and a clogged one.

Tap practice. Tap temperature for plain low-carbon rebar is 1610 to 1640 Celsius depending on tundish distance and ladle treatments needed. We measure with a disposable thermocouple right before tap — never trust the bath temperature reading alone, the meniscus is colder than the bulk by 15 to 25 degrees. Tap into the ladle through the EBT — eccentric bottom tap — at maximum 80 percent flow rate, never full open, because high tap stream causes ladle slag entrainment and oxygen pickup. We tap with argon shroud always — 60 to 100 normal cubic metres per hour through a refractory tube around the stream. Skip the shroud and you'll see nitrogen pickup from 40 ppm to over 100 ppm, which causes age cracking in finished rebar. The tap weight should be 75 to 78 tonnes — we keep 2 to 4 tonnes of hot heel for the next heat. Below 1.5 tonnes of heel and your bore-in is rough; above 5 tonnes and you're losing capacity for no benefit.

Hot heel management is what differentiates a Tier 1 mill from the rest. The heel is the molten steel left after tap that becomes your starting bath for the next charge. We check heel composition every shift by lance sampling — composition drift over 0.05 percent carbon between heats means your slag isn't doing its job and carbon is reverting from the heel into the new charge. Heel temperature should be between 1500 and 1530 at start of next charge — colder and the first scrap freezes against the bottom and you get a delay; hotter and you're burning energy you don't need. We put a thin slag cover on the heel during turnaround, about 50 millimetres of basic powder, to slow heat loss and prevent oxidation. If a heat finishes and you're not charging within 15 minutes, you should add carbon to the heel — 50 to 80 kilos — to suppress oxidation losses. Without that addition the heel surface oxidizes fast and you're effectively starting cold.

Off-gas energy. The off-gas system is where most plants leave 30 percent of input energy on the floor. Our system has post-combustion injectors — secondary oxygen lances pointed at the freeboard above the bath — that burn the CO from refining into CO2, releasing 280 megajoules per kilo of CO. We run 8 to 12 normal cubic metres per minute of secondary oxygen during the active CO evolution period. The signal that you're hitting the right ratio is a freeboard temperature of 1600 to 1700 Celsius — colder and you're not catching the CO; hotter and you're damaging the duct refractory. We instrumented the duct with thermocouples 2 metres downstream of the post-combustion zone — that's the cleanest measurement point. Heat recovery on this system saves us 25 to 30 kilowatt-hours per tonne, which on a 80-tonne heat is 2.4 megawatt-hours of grid power per heat. That's real money.

Refractory. The big knobs are bottom life, sidewall life, and roof life — different mechanisms, different lifespans. Bottom is shotcreted MgO-C with carbon binder, lifetime 8000 to 12000 heats depending on chemistry abuse. Sidewall above the slag line is water-cooled panels — those don't wear, but the refractory between panels does, that's about 800 to 1200 heats. Roof is the killer — typical life 200 to 400 heats and it depends entirely on how often you run with a naked arc. The hot spots on the roof are at 4 and 8 o'clock when looking down through the electrode triangle — those positions see the most arc radiation when foam coverage is thin. We do hot fettling — patching with MgO gunite — between heats during turnaround, focusing on those hot spots. Five to ten kilos per patch, 30 second application time, water spray to set it. If you're patching more than three times per shift the foam practice is wrong, fix that before fixing the refractory.

Electrode consumption is one of the most expensive variables in this shop. Standard graphite electrodes cost 4 to 5 thousand dollars per tonne and we consume about 1.6 to 2.0 kilos per tonne of liquid steel — that's 130 to 160 kilos per 80-tonne heat. Sidewall oxidation is 60 percent of consumption: graphite reacts with oxygen in the off-gas above the slag line. Tip wear from arc is the next 30 percent. Stub-end loss when you change electrodes is the last 10 percent. Reduce sidewall oxidation by injecting argon through the electrode column — what we call the cooling collar — 5 to 8 normal cubic metres per hour per electrode. Reduce tip wear with foamy slag practice. Reduce stub loss by changing electrodes only when remaining length is below 800 millimetres — premature changes cost you the residual graphite. Watch the electrode regulator chart — if you're moving electrodes more than 6 times per minute you're wearing them faster than necessary, the regulator gain is too aggressive.

If I had to give the most important rule to a new EAF foreman it's this: the slag is your dashboard. Every other indicator — temperature, current, voltage, power — is a lagging indicator. The slag tells you in real time what's happening in the bath. Colour, fluidity, the way it spits when carbon hits, how it flows when you tilt — those are leading indicators. The senior melters in this shop can tell you the FeO content within 2 percent just by looking at the slag colour through the slag door. Train your operators to read slag and you've trained them to run the furnace. The other thing is don't optimize for short-term productivity at the cost of refractory life. Pushing 100 megawatts on a thin foam to make tonnage saves 90 seconds per heat but costs you 50 heats of roof life. The math is brutal — that 90 seconds adds up to maybe 12 minutes of extra production, but the refractory bill is 8000 dollars per roof patch and a roof rebuild is 200,000 dollars. Slow down by 30 seconds, cover your arc, save the roof.
